dialogue.utterance: Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I have been having trouble breathing and coughing up frothy sputum this past week.
You mentioned coughing-can you describe the sputum and when the trouble breathing started?
The sputum is white and frothy, and the shortness of breath started about a week ago.
Has there been any chest pain related to it?
Only when I cough-then it feels like a sharp pain.
I see you're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Let's go ahead with the physical exam.
Your airway is open and clear. Are you able to speak normally without coughing now?
Your breathing is showing some signs of increased work-especially when you lie down.
I hear prolonged expiratory phase rales in your lungs, which could indicate fluid build-up.
Your radial pulses are strong and equal on both sides.
Skin appears warm and dry with normal color and no signs of cyanosis.
Your pulse is elevated at 103 beats per minute-this is sinus tachycardia on the ECG.
Respiratory rate is 23, with some labored breathing, but you're breathing air effectively for now.
Blood pressure is 141 over 83-your readings are stable at the moment.
Oxygen saturation is 98%-we'll monitor this closely as we proceed.
Blood glucose is high at 223-this aligns with your non-insulin-dependent diabetes.
We'll place you on the cardiac monitor to check for any issues.
We've taken a 12-lead ECG and saw a left bundle branch block pattern.
We will attempt an IV for fluid access-starting from the external jugular.
The IV access through the external jugular vein was unsuccessful after two attempts.
We've placed you on an etCO2 monitor for continuous respiratory tracking.
We're going to give you a nebulizer treatment with albuterol and ipratropium to help open your airways.
Pulse decreased to 91 after the medication.
Respiratory rate dropped to 11, with marked improvement in air flow.
Oxygen saturation rose to 99 after the nebulizer treatment.
Blood pressure is now 107 over 67-slightly lower but within a reasonable range.
I notice moderate, bilateral pitting edema in your legs. Is that consistent with what you've been experiencing?
Yes, it's been there since the morning, and it's sore to the touch-especially in the ankles.
Tell me more about how this has affected your daily activity recently.
I haven't been walking much due to fatigue and swelling-I've been trying to sit most of the time.
Your eyes are tracking normally and reacting well to light-no signs of jugular vein distension.
Chest rises and falls evenly. The rales in your lungs are in all lung fields and consistent on both sides.
Your abdomen is soft and without tenderness.
You have equal grip strength and mobility in both arms.
Both legs show +2 pitting edema and some tenderness. Are there any areas of numbness or tingling?
No numbness-just discomfort in my lower legs.
Can you tell me about any past medical issues you've had that may affect your breathing?
I have congestive heart failure and acute kidney failure. I'm on medication for that-was given more today at my doctor's office.
What happened today that caused you to call 911 besides the shortness of breath and cough?
I was given lasix this morning and didn't feel any better. I still have swelling and difficulty catching my breath at night.
When was the last time you had something to eat or drink?
I had some tea around noon, but I haven't been eating much.
The patient has no known drug allergies.
She is on Lantus, Albuterol, Lasix, and other medications such as Coreg and Avandaryl.
After medication, blood glucose is no longer measurable-possibly due to the IV access being unsuccessful.
The EKG remains showing left bundle branch block. No new signs of anterior wall MI noted.
She prefers to go to St. Mary's in Cold, as she's familiar with the staff there.
Your respiratory rate is up to 19 again-we noticed a drop in oxygen saturation just now.
Oxygen saturation is down to 60%-this is a concern.
Blood pressure is now 130 over 67-remains stable despite the recent drop in oxygen.
Heart rate is 92-still under control for now.
Left bundle branch block is still evident on the EKG.
Let me listen to your lungs again. The rales are persistent but no new crackles or wheezes were heard.
Radial pulses are still strong and equal. No signs of worsening perfusion.
We're going to give another round of nebulizer medication to see if things improve before we load you.
This is dosage two of albuterol and Atrovent via nebulizer mask.
You mentioned improvement in breathing after the first treatment-what about now?
I can definitely take in more air, but I'm still aware of the shortness of breath.
We're preparing the stretcher now. I'm going to ask you to help us stand and pivot you onto the stretcher.
You will remain in the Fowler's position with the five-point harness for safety during transport.
All equipment is in the ambulance now. We have the ECG and medications ready.
Hospital has been contacted with a summary, and we'll have you in room 13 for the ED.
We've been monitoring your status well-your vitals are stable overall.
Heart rate remains steady at 92-no new issues seen on the monitor.
Blood glucose still reads 0-ongoing monitoring recommended for hypoglycemia.
You mentioned a chest pain when you cough-how is this today?
It's a sharp pain, but not constant-only flares up with forceful coughing.
We'll do a final EKG as we approach the hospital for full records.
Ready when you are at hospital-transferring care now to receiving nurse.
This was managed under the respiratory distress protocol-medical determination is pulmonary edema onset with LBBB.
All monitoring ceased and care transferred now to ED room 13 staff.
